sections.heading: Relevancy of Facts
sections.body_text: Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other facts as are declared to be relevant under this Act, and of no others.

sections.heading: Facts forming part of the same transaction
sections.body_text: Facts which are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

sections.heading: Facts which are the occasion, cause, or effect of facts in issue
sections.body_text: Facts which are the occasion, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, are relevant.

sections.heading: Admissions defined
sections.body_text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary, which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons specified under this Act.

sections.heading: Admissions by parties to proceedings
sections.body_text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent authorized by such party, are admissions and are relevant against such party.

sections.heading: When oral admissions as to contents of documents are relevant
sections.body_text: Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant unless the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence of the contents of such document.

sections.heading: Confession caused by inducement, threat, or promise
sections.body_text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding if it appears to the court to have been caused by any inducement, threat, or promise.

sections.heading: Dying declaration
sections.body_text: Statements made by a person as to the cause of their death, or as to any of the circumstances of the transaction which resulted in their death, are relevant in cases where the cause of that person's death comes into question.
sections.chapter: Chapter IV - Statements by Persons Who Cannot Be Called as Witnesses

sections.heading: Confession of one accused affecting another
sections.body_text: When more persons than one are being tried jointly for the same offence, and a confession made by one of such persons affects the other, the court may take into consideration such confession as against the other accused.

sections.heading: Opinions of experts
sections.body_text: When the court has to form an opinion upon a point of foreign law, science, art, or as to the identity of handwriting or finger impressions, the opinions of persons skilled in such matters are relevant facts.
sections.chapter: Chapter V - Opinions of Third Persons

sections.heading: Opinion as to handwriting
sections.body_text: When the court has to form an opinion as to the person by whom any document was written or signed, the opinion of any person acquainted with the handwriting of the person by whom it is supposed to be written or signed is relevant.
sections.chapter: Chapter V - Opinions of Third Persons

sections.heading: Proof of facts by oral evidence
sections.body_text: All facts, except the contents of documents or electronic records, may be proved by oral evidence.

sections.heading: Oral evidence must be direct
sections.body_text: Oral evidence must, in all cases, be direct; that is to say, it must be the evidence of a witness who says he saw it, heard it, or perceived it by any other sense.

sections.heading: Proof of contents of documents
sections.body_text: The contents of documents may be proved either by primary or by secondary evidence.

sections.heading: Admissibility of electronic records
sections.body_text: Electronic records are admissible as evidence if they meet certain conditions, including the integrity of the electronic record and the reliability of the system used to produce it.

sections.heading: Cases in which secondary evidence relating to documents may be given
sections.body_text: Secondary evidence may be given of the existence, condition, or contents of a document in cases where the original is shown or appears to be in the possession of the opposite party or is lost or destroyed.

sections.heading: Proof of execution of document required by law to be attested
sections.body_text: If a document is required by law to be attested, it shall not be used as evidence unless at least one attesting witness has been called for the purpose of proving its execution.

sections.heading: Burden of proof
sections.body_text: Whoever desires any court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts, must prove that those facts exist.
sections.chapter: Chapter IX - Of the Burden of Proof

sections.heading: On whom burden of proof lies
sections.body_text: The burden of proof in a suit or proceeding lies on that person who would fail if no evidence at all were given on either side.
sections.chapter: Chapter IX - Of the Burden of Proof

sections.heading: Birth during marriage, conclusive proof of legitimacy
sections.body_text: The fact that any person was born during the continuance of a valid marriage between his mother and any man is conclusive proof that he is the legitimate child of that man, unless proven otherwise.
sections.chapter: Chapter IX - Of the Burden of Proof

sections.heading: Court may presume existence of certain facts
sections.body_text: The court may presume the existence of any fact which it thinks likely to have happened, regard being had to the common course of natural events, human conduct, and public and private business.
sections.chapter: Chapter IX - Of the Burden of Proof

sections.heading: Estoppel
sections.body_text: When one person has, by his declaration, act, or omission, intentionally caused or permitted another person to believe a thing to be true and to act upon such belief, he shall not be allowed to deny the truth of that thing.

sections.heading: Who may testify
sections.body_text: All persons shall be competent to testify unless the court considers that they are prevented from understanding the questions put to them due to tender years, extreme old age, disease, or any other cause.

sections.heading: Witness unable to communicate verbally
sections.body_text: A witness who is unable to speak may give evidence in any other manner in which he can make it intelligible, such as by writing or signs.

sections.heading: Evidence as to affairs of State
sections.body_text: No one shall be permitted to give evidence derived from unpublished official records relating to the affairs of State, except with the permission of the officer at the head of the department concerned.

sections.heading: Witness not excused from answering on ground that answer will incriminate
sections.body_text: A witness shall not be excused from answering any question on the ground that the answer will incriminate him, but no such answer shall subject him to arrest or prosecution.

sections.heading: Number of witnesses
sections.body_text: No particular number of witnesses shall in any case be required for the proof of any fact.

sections.heading: Examination-in-chief, cross-examination, and re-examination
sections.body_text: The examination of a witness by the party who calls him shall be called his examination-in-chief. The examination of a witness by the adverse party shall be called his cross-examination. The examination of a witness subsequent to the cross-examination by the party who called him shall be called his re-examination.
sections.chapter: Chapter XII - Of the Examination of Witnesses

sections.heading: Order of examinations
sections.body_text: Witnesses shall be first examined-in-chief, then cross-examined, and then, if the party calling him so desires, re-examined.
sections.chapter: Chapter XII - Of the Examination of Witnesses

sections.heading: Questions lawful in cross-examination
sections.body_text: When a witness is cross-examined, he may be asked any questions which tend to test his veracity, to discover who he is, or to shake his credit by injuring his character.
sections.chapter: Chapter XII - Of the Examination of Witnesses

sections.heading: Impeaching credit of witness
sections.body_text: The credit of a witness may be impeached by the adverse party by showing that he is unworthy of belief or by proving inconsistent statements made by him.
sections.chapter: Chapter XII - Of the Examination of Witnesses
